title: Globally representative evidence on the actual and perceived support for climate action
authors: Peter Andre, Teodora Boneva, Felix Chopra, Armin Falk

tax_mentions.exact_term_used_in_paper: a carbon tax on fossil fuels
tax_mentions.context: Validation sample: support for specific climate policies, including a carbon tax on fossil fuels, measured on Likert scale and correlated with general demand for more political action.
tax_mentions.page_or_section: Main text p.254; Methods – Validation; Supplementary Table 3
tax_mentions.notes: US-only validation data, not global sample.

excluded_policy_mentions.exact_term_used_in_paper: willing to contribute 1% of their household income every month to fight global warming
excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: Voluntary contribution framed as donation to fight warming, not a compulsory tax.
excluded_policy_mentions.page_or_section: Main text pp.253–255; Methods – Survey items (Willingness to contribute)

excluded_policy_mentions.exact_term_used_in_paper: national government should do more to fight global warming
excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: Measures general demand for more climate action without specifying instruments or taxes.
excluded_policy_mentions.page_or_section: Main text pp.254–255; Methods – Survey items (Demand for political action)

excluded_policy_mentions.exact_term_used_in_paper: people in their country should try to fight global warming
excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: Injunctive norm about individual efforts against warming, not a policy or tax measure.
excluded_policy_mentions.page_or_section: Main text p.254; Methods – Survey items (Social norms)

inclusion_reason: Includes validation analysis using a US survey where support for specific climate policies, including a carbon tax on fossil fuels, is measured and correlated with general climate-action demand.
